chapters.title: Don’t fear the quirky questions
chapters.text:
Anxiety often feels like a cage in the middle of a fog, trapping our minds with terrifying thoughts that peek at us from the shadows. We don't know if they bring real danger, but we can't stop noticing them out of the corners of our eyes.

Anxiety can feel like a state of perpetual drowning. You don't know when, but it could happen anytime.

Martha Beck knows these feelings all too well. As a youth, she was prescribed Valium for her anxious feelings; that's how strong they were. And she tried to fight her irrational fears for many of her adult years. However, the answer was not in escaping them but following something on the opposite side of anxiety — curiosity.

What if your worries and doubts are signs that your mind wants and can do more if you know how to allow it? Being curious takes a lot of courage, but once you follow your squirrels (more on that later), intuition, and creativity, your anxiety will slowly take a back seat.

Ready to welcome a calmer, less anxious lifestyle? Join us on this journey, and find out!

chapters.title: This is not a drill; our brains are hacked!
chapters.text:
Not many things are as frustrating as working hard, yet being unable to enjoy the fruits of our labor. It's not that we don't want to, but our fear of failing keeps us in a hamster wheel.

In 2023, 50% of the population had an anxiety diagnosis. While international affairs and climate change have influenced this statistic, the main culprit is the lifestyle many of us were born into. Let's look at where it all began — the brain.

The brain's right hemisphere deals with emotions, intuition, and sensations, while the left deals with logic and analytical thinking. Moreover, the left half really loves control. Hustle culture made us overly reliant on the left hemisphere. When an employee is alert, ready to take on one more task, and works twice as hard, profit follows. Operating at peak capacity forces the left half to juggle several jobs, hobbies, and contacts, all in the fear of losing everything.

Many of us were born in the bloom of this culture, so we don't know any better. When Beck suggested one of her client's anxiety was a perfectly natural response to her unhealthy lifestyle, the client was dumbstruck.

You see, the client was a successful young woman working at a law firm and the first college graduate in her family. Not only that, but she had to constantly prove her worth in a male-dominated field, which added invisible stress. Isn't it worrying that we sometimes don't think this is already too much?

The value of our leisure today depends on how much it contributes to the workforce. But you can CHOOSE differently.

Biological anthropologist Joseph Henrich coined a term for this lifestyle — WEIRD, meaning Western, Educated, Industrialized, Rich, and Democratic. Starting a few hundred years ago in Western Europe, this mindset rejected the spiritual, focusing on the material and everything we could do to it — measure, analyze, calculate, and label. All these functions are possible due to the left hemisphere's work. Ultimately, we overworked the left so much that we now suffer because of it.
Luckily, we still have a right hemisphere and a malleable brain to heal ourselves. Let's find out how through Beck's advice and a practical action plan with tips we've sprinkled throughout this summary.

chapters.title: Your anxiety is a scared child
chapters.text:
We’ve heard a lot about the left hemisphere, but what about the right one? As it turns out, anxiety predominantly targets the left side of the brain — that’s why we feel compelled to logically explain every trigger.

Little story time: Beck's friend Jennifer once stayed at a reserve in South Africa. One night, she woke to a leopard munching on a dead animal. Her amygdala reacted immediately — she wrapped herself in a blanket so that the predator wouldn't perceive her as a rival. In the morning, Jennifer laughed at how ridiculous her thinking was, but her left hemisphere saved her from danger that night. For those with anxiety, this constant sense of danger is all too familiar.

Unfortunately, our brains are evolutionarily wired to prioritize negative memories, which triggers anxiety more often than joy.

Consider another story: one morning, a vein in Jill Bolte Taylor's brain ruptured, filling its left side with blood. Taylor, a neuroanatomist, temporarily lost the ability to speak, reason, and track linear time. Her right hemisphere took the wheel, waking Taylor’s will to paint. The urge to create was so strong and freeing that she decided to stimulate her right side deliberately.

The mind cannot create when all its resources go into survival mode.

So, how do we reduce our anxious feelings by stimulating the right hemisphere (intuition and imagination)? Imagining or recalling your safe place, like your warm bed, or a pleasant smell, like the scent of a rose, can help push your anxiety away. It's how you get your right hemisphere working! Now, imagine your anxiety is an upset child. You must soothe it. Whisper to it and shift its mind to its imagination. Try this calming technique:

1. Release a long, slow exhale. This will slow your heartbeat and weaken your fight-or-flight response.
2. Soften your eye focus to send your body a signal of safety.
3. Let your anxiety roam. Punch a pillow, pace the room, or even tremble if necessary.
4. Accept your anxiety. If you can't, accept its presence and let it roam.
5. Hum, murmur, or sing something like you would to calm a child. Your amygdala reacts to relaxed sounds and vibrations.
6. Talk kindly to yourself. Say things like: "You're OK," "I see you," and "I'm here with you."
7. Send yourself warm wishes: "May you feel happy," "May you feel safe," "May you feel peaceful."

chapters.title: Your triggers are playing hard to get
chapters.text:
You now know how to temporarily whisper away your anxiety. But will it return with the next trigger?

As tools of emotional regulation, triggers often point to traumatic experiences from our earlier lives. For example, consider Jim, who saw his drinking buddy swirling his glass of scotch around — just as his stepdad did before becoming violent — and felt the sudden urge to punch him. Jim’s first reaction was to fight, while others might freeze, flee, appease, or shut down. Psychologists refer to these reactions as the five “Fs”: fight, flight, fawn, freeze, and flop.

Pacifying a narcissistic parent is an example of fawning that can end in people pleasing. And have you ever felt scared to go to work after getting promoted? That's flopping — your whole body weakens, and you call in sick. Sometimes a child flops when extra effort is needed to excel at a school project, and as that child grows up, similar challenges can become anxiety triggers. So how do we break this cycle?

Your triggers grow with you. Look for their roots in your childhood.

Quitting negative thoughts triggered by a painful event or person is difficult — our entire body screams "CODE RED!" Some psychology experts even built a color-coded setup where:

• The green-light situation is our comfort zone.
• The yellow-light situation is us sensing danger.
• The red-light situation is fatalistic — "There's no way out!"

Beck combined this scheme with how animal trainers calm zoo inhabitants, synthesizing her own peace on the battlefield with her triggered mind. You can try her routine to see if it fits you:
1. Fill your safe space — like your bedroom — with soothing objects. It can be a blanket, your favorite book or vinyl, or a cup of your favorite drink.
2. Enter your green-light state by focusing on these objects. Notice your feelings as you do so.
3. Think of something that moves you to the yellow area. Starting that student paper or making a call can be a good start.
4. Return to the green light using the calming technique from the previous key point.
5. Now try thinking of the red-light situation — your upcoming exam, work deadline, or recent fight with your significant other.
6. Practice surrendering and kind self-talk to return to the green light zone safely. Repeat this exercise regularly.

Hopefully, you feel green now, but there’s still more to come in the Try This section. Are you ready?

chapters.title: Curiosity is the opposite of anxiety
chapters.text:
We've talked enough about calming our anxiety. And most books and articles usually stop at this, according to Beck. However, she wanted to proceed into her creative self, which meant working even more with her right hemisphere.

Anxiety pulls us away from discoveries. Creativity leads us to them.

Talking with some of her creative clients, Beck found that they never felt anxious when working. However, anxiety often kept them away from creating. Little did these creatives know — they had a solution in their hands. If anxiety is about avoiding fear, then curiosity is about going toward it and away from anxiety.

Psychologist Jordan Litman differentiates two types of curiosity — "deprivation curiosity," which is driven by survival ("How will I afford food and shelter?"), and "interest curiosity," separate from work tasks or studies. In other words, it's pure curiosity.

In the early 2000s, product designer Peter Skillman tasked different-aged groups, kindergarteners to business school students, to construct a freestanding tower using dry spaghetti and other tools. The preschoolers won the challenge, fueled by their interest curiosity, while the older students failed.

Notice how young children ask "What's this?" about almost everything while adults do not. Why? Adults limit their imagination to optimal work tasks, while children’s imagination is unlimited. At some point, Beck found out she had an "abnormally interest-based nervous system," also known as ADHD. Doctors seriously suggested treating her, to which she laughed. Instead, she started painting to satiate her right hemisphere.

Just as anxiety is the end product of a paranoid imagination, inspiration is the end product of a creative imagination. ~ Martha Beck

If you decide to start creating, don't worry if your urge to paint, write, dance, or whatever you choose is persistent. All the years of repression seek release, so allow yourself to create extensively.

Soon enough, your left and right hemispheres will find a balance, so you can participate in your job and be an artist or simply a curious person. None of those things are mutually exclusive.

Did you know? Gen Z is expected to comprise 30% of the workforce in 2025, and their key expectations include clear work-leisure boundaries, a sense of belonging, and equitable salaries, according to the 2023 study at Bryant University.

chapters.title: Follow your squirrels
chapters.text:
Beck once worked with a woman named Frieda, whose acute anxiety made her life as challenging as it gets. Her service dog, Griffin, was her complete opposite, sitting calmly. His only weakness was squirrels, never missing the chance to chase one happily. It often seemed that this disciplined dog was trying to show Frieda that beyond fear, there is also joy. Griffin's passion was chasing squirrels, and his wish was to show Frieda that despite fears, passions are also all around her.

We often confuse passion with lightning that strikes our hearts on a clear day. In truth, it starts with a nibble of curiosity that slowly grows and becomes passion. Children follow everything that piques their attention because they're driven by "What's this?" and not by "I don't have time for this."

There's no shame in being passionate about something. The real shame is us allowing others to decide what we like.

It takes a bit more time and effort as an adult, but you can rediscover things that once ignited your inner Griffin:
1. Find your anti-squirrel: Recall stuff you didn't enjoy at school, like reading textbooks, boring PowerPoint presentations, or a specific topic. How did your body feel thinking of them?
2. Find your squirrel: Now repeat it, but with things you enjoyed. How did you feel paying full attention to them?
3. Notice your feelings going through your typical day: Which things feel like squirrels and which feel like anti-squirrels?

The more you apply this thinking to surrounding things, the easier it becomes to identify what sparks your curiosity. Beck even came up with something like "sanity quilts," made of everything that made you say, "Oh, that's cool!" or "Wish I could do that!" and "I have to learn that!" You can add new threads to your sanity quilt by trying out new hobbies, going to new places, or doing things you didn't dare to do before. Use this “quilt” whenever anxiety is overtaking you.

Last but not least — if you can't start working on an idea, your mind and body are likely exhausted after all the bill-paying work. Taking less work when possible, resting without social media, and getting good sleep and food for as few as four days are enough to rejuvenate.

chapters.title: You actually CAN do what you want
chapters.text:
The WEIRD Western world has committed one unforgivable sin — it made people orbit their jobs. Many people with corporate jobs have developed "work-based identities," unable to leave their companies because they can't imagine themselves as just individuals.

Tragically, these same people often want to leave, scared to think it's all their lives can be. Not only are they disconnected from their right hemisphere, but they also live in a culture that claims to know what's best for them. This belief makes it hard to leave the certainty of the corporate world that rewards practice with quantifiable results.

There are too many things in the world for one lifetime; trying to know them all means skipping on actual life.

Do you often feel like you could be doing something other than your work? If so, your purpose is bigger than your role as an employee. It isn't easy to leave a well-paying job, but it's possible to start a creative “side quest” right where you are. You may want to try this:

• Divide a piece of paper into two columns.
• In the first column, complete the sentence: I never/always [activity]. If I did/didn't, people would think I'm [judgment]. For example, I never stand up for myself. If I did, people would think I'm selfish.
• In the second column, write things you'd prefer to do next to each statement. For example, I'd like to ask for a raise.
• Continue adding I never/always and If I did/didn't sentences as you think of them.
• Each day, choose one action to do from the second column. Repeat until all actions are done.

Still, there are times when people don't know what they’d like to do, which is another reason they don't leave their jobs. If you find this familiar, how about looking at people you look up to? Whether musicians, athletes, or professional yappers on YouTube, if they light the bulb of your curiosity ("Oh, what's that? I wanna try this!"), why not give yourself a chance?

If the thought of leaving the shore makes you anxious, return to the exercises from the first key point or try these four things:

1. Go up: Imagine you're a hawk flying high above, searching for the ultimate person inside you who can do the scary thing.
2. Go down: Having realized there’s no one else, return and focus on that scary task.
3. Go away: Or do something completely different, like take a walk, read a book, or lay in silence.
4. Eventually, go back to the task.

chapters.title: Move to your own ecosystem
chapters.text:
Has your job ever stopped you from doing something you can't stop thinking about? Your answer is probably "yes," isn't it?

You already heard about the WEIRD culture — the Western model of prosperity that puts growth and profit above all else. What happens when something grows uncontrollably? It starts to absorb its surroundings and, eventually, itself — just like cancer cells.

Max Weber, the "father of sociology," noticed this carcinogenic hint in the American economy more than a hundred years ago. The builders of this economy were White Catholic men who believed that wealth comes only to the righteous — meaning them — so now it requires ever-expanding wealth to support itself. If a current economic state could be an ecosystem, it would be a really unsustainable one. What's worse — it would be completely uninhabitable for our beautiful minds.

Having realized that, Beck started working toward her own ecosystem that would fertilize her talents and let them flourish instead of diminishing. To put it simply, you can turn your interest into a source of income as long as you keep battling your anxiety.

... Humans aren't naturally built to depend on money; we're built to depend on biological systems. Because we are biological systems. ~ Martha Beck

Because the world is oversaturated with left-brained people, those relying more on their right hemisphere are becoming more popular among employers for their creative approaches. Don't let these words trouble you — this division doesn't make someone better or worse. Rather, it means your creativity can secure your life financially without succumbing to the grind.

You can try this technique to start a mental place for your ecosystem:

• Go into your green-light state.
• While relaxed, imagine a piece of nature that appeals to you, such as the ocean or a deep forest.
• Try to picture yourself as this place.
• Imagine your ideal life three or four years in the future if finances were not a problem.
• If your anxiety starts rising ("How will I get there?" "It's impossible"), pay attention to it and calm it.
• Meditate like this every morning and evening as you start taking small steps toward your more fulfilling life.

Words always come before actions, so mind what you say to yourself.

chapters.title: Try this
chapters.text:
Creative discovery doesn't have to cause anxiety, especially when starting something new.

• Take the "spark" test: Write down ten things you would like to try despite how impractical they seem. Underline the three that excite you the most and try them this month.
• Practice the five "whys": Stop yourself at some point in your day and ask yourself "why." Why am I doing this? Why does my day look like this? Why do I want to do that specific thing?
• Give your interest 20 minutes a day: Set aside as little as 20 minutes to research your interest, build a plan to learn more, or play with something from your sanity quilt.
• Care for your inner child: Close your eyes and picture your inner child. How old are they? What makes them anxious right now? Where do you sense this anxiety in your body? Ask, "What can I do to help you?" Listen to your inner child intently. They may want to doodle, play outside, or watch a cartoon — whatever calms a little kid.
• Track your energy: Throughout the week, take notes of what makes you energized, exhausted, scared, and anxious. Use this "map" to exclude activities or places that cut your energy.

These exercises can inspire your change, but your main task is to ban yourself from doing what doesn't fulfill you. Your mind is too valuable for serving ideas that are not your own.

Read it till the end?
